title: Greece Civilization_Politics (By Aristotle)
paragraphs: In the Ethics, he has described the character necessary for the good life. However, he argues that this life is essentially lived in society. When he comes to the practical application of his inquiries in the last chapters of the Ethics, he finds expression not in moral exhortations addressed to the individual, but rather in a description of the legislative opportunities available to the statesman. His ideal city is, as he says, a pattern laid up in heaven by which the just man may rule his life. This pattern serves as a guide for the individual, rather than the statesman.

It may help to understand politics if something is said about those two points. However, the maxim that applies to modern nation-states does not apply to the smaller and more self-conscious Greek city. The situation described likely occurs frequently in real life.

It is in the state where common seeking after the good, which is the deepest truth about men and nature, becomes explicit and knows itself. The state comes before the family and the village, but they precede it in time. It is only when a state with a conscious organization is established that man can comprehend the mystery of his past struggles. If a primitive society is understood in terms of the state, then the state can be understood in terms of its most perfected form, where the good that all societies strive for is realized in its fullness.

In accordance with the same line of thought, imperfect states are regarded by Aristotle as the result not of perverse will, but rather of misconception and ignorance. The cure for political ills is knowledge of the good life, and the statesman is he who possesses such knowledge, for it alone can provide men with what they are always seeking. His citizens, therefore, must be men of leisure, capable of rational thought on the end of life.

Those who possess complete knowledge of the good must be few. Consequently, Plato entrusted the entire power of his state to a small minority of philosopher-guards. The analogy between the legislator and the craftsman, as Plato insists, collapses because the legislator deals with individuals like himself, people who can to some extent grasp their own purpose in life and cannot be viewed solely as a means to achieve the legislator's goal. Men have common aspirations, but government and political power are necessary because men's community is imperfect. This is due to man's social nature, which expresses itself in conflicting ways, such as the clash of interests, rivalry among parties, and struggle between classes. Instead of unity in seeking a common good, there is division.

Plato and Aristotle were familiar with the legislator who was elected by the whole people. They therefore took the general will or common consent of the people as a given. Aristotle views existing constitutions as imperfect approximations to the ideal, but also sees them as the outcome of a class struggle. The causes of revolutions are not primarily described as changes in the conception of the common good, but rather as changes in the military or economic power of the various classes within a state.

He therefore considered them as natural slaves. Aristotle views nature as fixed and immutable. He therefore supports the institution of slavery, which posits that a person's inherent characteristics determine their identity and limits their potential for change. The notion of a city naturally precedes that of a family or an individual. For the whole must necessarily be prior to the parts. If you take away the whole man, you cannot say a foot or a hand remains, unless by equivocation. However, supposing a hand made of stone would only result in a dead one. In reality, everything is understood to possess its unique qualities and powers. When these cease to exist, it's clear that the entity cannot be the same; rather, something bearing the same name remains.

Some people believe that the master's power over their slave comes from his superior knowledge, and that this knowledge is shared by the master, magistrate, and king, as we have already stated. Others argue that hierarchical government is unnatural and that it is the law that makes one man a slave and another free. However, they claim that in nature, there is no inherent difference between individuals, which means that power cannot be based on justice, but rather on force. A possession may be considered in the same nature as a part of anything. A part is not only a part of something, but also has no existence outside of it. Similarly, a possession has no separate identity beyond its relationship to what it possesses. Therefore, a master's authority over a slave is limited to their ownership, but not to any inherent connection with the slave as an individual. Conversely, a slave's servitude is solely bound to their relationship with their master, with no independent existence outside of that bond. CHAPTER V

But whether any person is by nature a slave or not, and whether it is advantageous and just for anyone to be a slave, shall be considered hereafter. It is not difficult to determine this on general principles or from matters of fact. Some people are born to govern, while others are suited to being governed. This natural division is both necessary and useful. From birth, some individuals are destined for leadership roles, while others are better suited to serving others.

Men who are inferior to others as much as the body is to the soul should be treated accordingly, since their proper use lies in their bodies, which are the source of their excellence. If what I have said is true, then they are born slaves and it is advantageous for them to be under government at all times. It is clear from what has been discussed that a hereditary and a political government are not the same, nor do they share similarities with one another. One type is suited to the nature of free men, while the other is designed for those who are not free. The master is not so called from his knowledge of managing his slave, but because he is one. For the same reason, a slave and a freeman have their respective names.

For what reason do others strive to acquire other riches and property? There are indeed other riches and property in nature, and these are the proper objects of economy. Trade, on the other hand, only procures money, not always, but through exchange. This is its primary purpose, as money serves as the foundation and ultimate goal of trade. It is indeed true that making a monopoly of anything can be beneficial for an individual. Some cities also employ this strategy when seeking to acquire wealth by controlling their commodities. Those who deny reason to slaves and claim they only need to follow orders are in the wrong. Slaves require more guidance than children do, which is why we must address this issue.

A city cannot be one in the way some people suggest. What was previously considered the greatest benefit a city could have is actually its downfall, as this would inevitably lead to its demise. The true good of something lies in preserving it. It's clear that another reason supports the idea that making a city too homogeneous isn't ideal. A family is more self-sufficient on its own than a single individual, and similarly, a city is more complete with a diverse population than a single family unit. In fact, Plato believed that a city's existence relies on the sufficiency of its members, which they enjoy within themselves. Those who have their wives and children in common will not say so, but everyone will say so, though not as individuals. Therefore, to use the word everyone is evidently a fallacious mode of speech; for this word is sometimes used distributively, and sometimes collectively, on account of its double meaning, which can lead to inconclusive syllogisms in reasoning.

For all persons to say the same thing would be well, but is impossible. In its collective sense, the word "all" would not contribute to the concord of the state. It is also absurd for those who promote a community of children to forbid those who love each other from indulging themselves in the last excesses of that passion, while they do not restrain them from the passion itself, or those intercourses which are of all things most improper, between a father and son, brother and brother. It is also absurd to prevent this relationship between close relatives, for no other reason than the intensity of the pleasure, while they believe that the relationship between a parent and child, or siblings, holds no significance.

It seems more advantageous for the state that husbandmen have their wives and children in common with them rather than the military. In this case, there will be less affection among them compared to when they are apart; such persons should be under subjection so they may obey the laws and not seek after innovations. People should be united and form a community through education. In ancient Sparta, for example, property was shared among citizens, and public meals were held in common, demonstrating the importance of communal living. Anyone who introduces education with the intention of making their city excellent and respectable is being absurd if they believe that such regulations alone can achieve this without also cultivating good manners, philosophy, and laws.

If he makes his wives common, while the property remains separate, who will manage the household affairs with the same diligence that a good farmer devotes to his fields? Man and woman are not equal in their relationships. Making property and marriage common will not resolve the issue. It's illogical to compare human connections to those of animals, expecting the same principles to apply. Happiness is not like numerical equality, which arises from certain numbers when added together. However, neither of these numbers can separately contain happiness on their own. Happiness cannot be added together in the same way that numbers can; it must exist within every individual, just as some properties belong to all things by definition. If the military are unhappy, who else is? The artisans are not for the multitude of those who are employed in inferior offices. The state that Socrates has described has all these defects, and others that are no less consequential.

CHAPTER VI It is also nearly the same in the treatise on Laws that was written later. For this reason, it will be proper in this place to briefly consider what he has said about government in that work, as Socrates has thoroughly settled only a few aspects of it. He discusses how community should be regulated among wives and children, how property should be established, and how government should be conducted. He divides the inhabitants into two parts: husbandmen and soldiers. From these, he selects a third part who are to be senators and govern the city. However, he has not specified whether the husbandmen and artisans will have any share in the government or if they will possess arms and participate in wars alongside the others. A legislator should consider two main factors when framing laws: the country's needs and the well-being of its citizens.

It may also be considered whether the quantity of each person's property should be settled in a different manner from what has been done previously. For he argues that everyone ought to have enough to live modestly, as if anyone were to say they must live well, which is the most comprehensive expression. A man can live moderately and miserably at the same time. Therefore, it would be better if they had proposed living both moderately and liberally. For unless these two ways of life coincide, luxury will inevitably follow one path or wretchedness the other, as these are the only two options for managing our resources. We cannot say a man is mild or courageous based on his fortune, but we can say he is prudent and liberal, which are the qualities most closely tied to it. Phidon the Corinthian, one of the oldest legislators, believed that families and the number of citizens should maintain the same proportion. However, he recognized that initially, each citizen would receive an allotment disproportionate to their actual numbers.

It is different in Plato's Laws. We will discuss this further. It is also said in this treatise [1266a] Laws, that the best form of government must be one composed of a democracy and a tyranny. However, such a mixture is not allowed by anyone else to exist as a government at all, or if it does, it would be the worst possible. Those who propose alternative forms suggest that blending many governments together produces the most perfect system. The principle of choosing their senate also prevails in the manner of electing them. This method is favorable to an oligarchy, as all must vote for those who are members of the first class, then for the same number from the second class, and finally from the third class. However, this compulsion to vote at senatorial elections does not apply to the lower classes. Only the first and second classes are required to vote for the fourth.

For this reason, Phaleas the Chalcedonian first proposed that the fortunes of citizens should be equal. He believed it was not difficult to achieve when a community was first settled, but more challenging in an established one. However, he argued that equality could still be introduced through certain means. The rich should provide marriage portions without ever receiving any, while the poor should always receive but never give anything in return. Legislators who would establish this principle are likely to overlook what they should consider: while regulating the quantity of provisions each individual shall possess, they must also regulate the number of their children. For if these numbers exceed the allotted quantity of provision, the law will be rendered null and void; yet, even after repeal, it will still have a detrimental effect, reducing many from wealth to poverty, as innovators are prone to making such mistakes. He should have specified his educational goals. This excessive focus on education can lead citizens to become overly ambitious, striving for honors, wealth, or both.

He has also been very desirous to establish rules that will contribute to perfecting his state's internal policy, and he ought also to have done the same with respect to its neighbors and all foreign nations. The considerations for military establishment should take place in planning every government, ensuring it is prepared in case of war. He has said nothing about this. Similarly, property should not only be adapted to the state's needs but also to potential dangers arising from outside. This man was in other respects too eager after notice. He seemed to many to live in an affected manner, characterized by his flowing locks and expensive ornaments. A coarse, warm vest was his constant attire, worn during both winter and hot weather. Besides what can prevent confusion on the bench when one judge thinks a fine should be different from what another has set it at? One proposes twenty minae, while another suggests ten or more or less. Another judge proposes four, and another five. It is evident that in this manner they will differ among themselves. Some will give the whole damages sued for, while others will give nothing. In such a situation, how shall their determinations be settled?

He who condemns him is guilty of perjury if he sentences him to pay twenty minae when he believes the damages should not be so much. Now, with respect to these honors which he proposes to bestow on those who can provide useful information to the community, this, though pleasing in speculation, is what the legislator should not settle, for it would encourage informers and probably occasion commotions in the state. It is possible to propose both laws and government as a common good. Since we have discussed this subject, it would be proper to delve into a few particulars regarding it. This area contains some difficulties, which I have already mentioned. Altering these difficulties may appear beneficial, as they have been found useful in other sciences. But no such thing has ever happened to the Cretans; the reason for which probably is, that although they are engaged in frequent wars with the neighbouring cities, yet none of these would enter into an alliance with the revolters, as it would be disadvantageous for them, who themselves also have their own villains.

The slaves of the Thessalians rose up in revolt while they were at war with their neighboring tribes, the Achaeans, Perrhaeans, and Magnesians. The origin of this indulgence, enjoyed by Lacedaemonian women, is easily explained. During a long period when men were frequently absent from home on foreign expeditions against the Argives and later the Arcadians and Messenians, their military life, which embodies virtue, prepared them to obey the laws laid down by their lawgiver. However, it was Lycurgus who attempted to impose his will on women, but they refused to comply, leading him to abandon his efforts. It may indeed be said that the women were the cause of these things.

We should not be focusing on where the fault lies or where it does not lie, but rather on what is right and wrong. When a woman's behavior is not properly regulated, as I have already stated, it can lead to faults that are shameful for the state and also fuel an excessive love of money. He made it infamous for anyone to buy or sell their possessions, a decision he had done right. However, he allowed anyone who chose to do so to give them away or leave them in a will, despite similar consequences arising from both practices. It appears  The text is already clear and concise. However, I did notice a potential issue with the logical sequence of numbers. It seems that the country has 15,000 horses and 30,000 soldiers (foot), but the total number mentioned is 1,000. This could be an error or a typo. If we assume that the original intention was to mention a different unit or quantity, I would not attempt to correct it without further context.

They say that during the reigns of their ancient kings, foreigners were presented with the freedom of their city to prevent a shortage of men while they waged long wars. It is also claimed that the number of Spartans was once ten thousand. However, regardless of this, an equality of property contributes significantly to increasing the population. The law, too, which he made to encourage population, was not calculated to correct this inequality. He wanted the Spartans to be as numerous as possible, making them desirous of having large families. To achieve this, he exempted those with three children from night-watch duty and those with four children from paying taxes. However, it is evident that if the people increased in number, many would inevitably become very poor. These men, corrupted by wealth, went as far as they could to destroy the city. Their immense power, bordering on tyranny, forced their rulers to appease them, which ultimately harmed the state. As a result, the system shifted from an aristocratic rule to a democratic one.

They may have a check on all the magistrates. To make his citizens ambitious in honor, he has filled his senate with men of that disposition, as no one else would seek the office. Despite this, the main cause of deliberate wrongdoing among men lies in ambition and greed. The public revenue was poorly managed at Sparta. The state's value diminished during their prolonged wars, and the subsidies were inadequately raised. This was due to the fact that the Spartans controlled a vast territory, which led to disagreements among them regarding their individual contributions.

Minos acquired the empire of the sea and its surrounding islands. He conquered some, established colonies on others, and eventually died at Camicus while attempting to conquer Sicily. There was once a kingly power in Crete, but it later dissolved, and control over their armies was given to the kosmoi. A state like this would never be exposed to invasions by those who were powerful and inclined to attack it. However, its situation preserves it, as it is free from foreign inroads. This freedom allows the family slaves on Crete to remain quiet, unlike the Helots, who perpetually revolt. The Cretans take no part in foreign affairs, and it was only recently that any foreign troops made an attempt to attack the island. Their ravages soon proved the ineffectiveness of their laws.

Amongst many excellent constitutions, this may show how well their government is framed. That although the people are admitted to a share in administration, the form of it remains unaltered, without any notable popular insurrections, or degenerating into tyranny on either hand. The five who elect each other possess great and extensive powers. They choose the hundred, who serve as magistrates of the highest rank. Their power lasts longer than that of any other magistrates, as it begins before they assume office and continues after they leave. This arrangement tends to favor an oligarchy. However, since they are not elected by lottery but rather by popular vote, and are prohibited from accepting bribes, they become strong advocates for an aristocracy. It was clear that this was not Solon's intention, but rather an accident. The people, having caused the naval victory over the Medes, took great credit for it and enlisted themselves under factious demagogues, despite being opposed by the majority of the citizens.

Some persons endeavour to prove that Onomacritus, the Locrian, was the first person of note who drew up laws. They claim he did so while at Crete, where he spent some time learning the prophetic art. According to them, Thales was his companion, and Lycurgus and Zaleucus were scholars of Thales, with Charondas being a scholar of Zaleucus. However, those who support this argument contradict chronology. There is a dispute about this. Some people claim the city was responsible for certain actions, while others argue it was the oligarchy or the tyrant who was to blame. It is not residence that makes a man a citizen. Sojourners and slaves are treated equally with him. Having the privilege of the laws will not be enough for this purpose. Even those from different nations who have agreed on it are allowed to plead or be sued, regardless of their nationality. However, it often happens that sojourners do not have a complete right to participate in the community without the protection of a patron, whom they must turn to for assistance. This indicates that their share in the community is incomplete.

All things that differ in kind share only a small amount in common after the initial two components are removed. Governments vary in their form, with some being flawed and others nearly perfect. It's clear that those with numerous shortcomings and weaknesses will always lag behind those with fewer flaws. It is likely even more challenging to establish their rights as citizens who have been granted freedom following any revolution within the state.

As an example, at Athens, after the expulsion of the tyrants, Clisthenes enrolled many foreigners and city slaves among the tribes. The question then arose as to whether they were citizens by law or not. If  There is a government where men govern those equal in rank. This is called a political government, where individuals learn to command by first submitting to obey. A good general or commander-in-chief must acquire knowledge of their duty by being under the command of another. Similarly, in every appointment within the army, it is said that no one knows how to command who has not been under command themselves.

Direction and knowledge of public affairs is a virtue peculiar to those who govern. Those who do not govern, however, seem equally necessary for both parties. But this does not concern the governed, as it is their duty to form their own opinions. They are like flute-makers, while those who govern are the musicians who play on them. Those who work for a single employer are slaves. Those who do so for pay are considered mechanics or hired hands. It becomes clear upon even slight consideration that this is their situation, as my previous explanation is supported by the evidence of appearance. In an oligarchy, only free-born citizens are admitted to citizenship. This is because the right to hold public office is determined by one's wealth, not birthright. However, this does not apply to all citizens, as many of them possess significant financial resources. There was a law at Thebes that no one could have a share in the government until he had been ten years out of trade.

There are various types of citizens, and it can be argued that one who shares the honors of the state is as complete as anyone else. This is evident from what has already been discussed. Formerly, it was natural for each citizen to serve the public and administer to his private good, just as he had done when in office for others. However, now everyone is eager to hold power continually, so they can enjoy the benefits of public business and being in office. This leads them to believe that places are a never-failing remedy for every complaint, and thus they are eagerly sought after. We typically refer to a state ruled by one person for the common good as a kingdom. One that is governed by more than one but only a few, is an aristocracy; either because the government is in the hands of the most worthy citizens or because it is the best form for the city and its inhabitants. A tyranny, as has been said, is a monarchy in which one person holds absolute and despotic power over the entire community and all its members. An oligarchy, on the other hand, is characterized by the concentration of state power in the hands of the wealthy. In contrast, a democracy is where those who are least deserving or have the least value are given the right to participate.

If (Note: No correction or enhancement can be made as ) In a society where the majority are affluent and the minority are impoverished (each possessing great power), assuming only two types of states exist as described. And why Because their mutual intercourse is not near enough, even if persons so situated come to one place, and everyone lives in his own house as in his native city, alliances would exist between each party to mutually assist and prevent any injury from being done to the other. However, those who think correctly would still deny them city status, assuming they preserve their customs when together as they do when separate.

It is not therefore founded solely for the purpose of men's mere coexistence, but for their living as they ought. Those who contribute most to this end deserve greater power in the city than those who are equal to them in family and freedom, but inferior in civil virtue, or excel them in wealth but are below them in worth. All parties in a dispute about government say something just. For what They may then divide among themselves what belongs to the rich. Nor is this unjust, for it has been so judged by the supreme power.

The people in some states are not exempt from what I have said. It appears you haven't provided any text to correct. The power of election and censure is now of utmost importance. In some states, this authority is vested in the people. The general assembly serves as the supreme court for all matters, and its members have a voice in all public affairs. They deliberate on all issues and preside over trials without exception, regardless of their financial circumstances or age. However, their treasurers, generals, and other high-ranking officials are typically drawn from individuals of great wealth and character.

CHAPTER XIII It seems, then, that establishing a state requires thoroughly examining or investigating many of these particulars. Virtue and education should be considered essential in making citizens happy, as previously discussed. All governments that assume equality in one area do not necessarily possess equality in all areas, nor do those that acknowledge inequality in one area necessarily lack it in others. It logically follows that any government founded on the assumption of equality in all respects is flawed. We have already stated that all members of the community will dispute with each other over state offices. In some particulars, this is justifiable, but not in general. The rich, for example, are entitled to dispute due to their significant landed property and ultimate claim to the soil being vested in the community. Additionally, their loyalty is generally most dependable.

When everything happens at once and we must live in the same city. And upon this principle, too, while many think they should have the supreme command because they are more powerful than the few, if only one or more than one is found stronger than them, then those who are stronger should have it, not they. The rectitude of anything consists in its equality. Therefore, what is equally just will be advantageous to the whole state and to each of its members alike.

A citizen is one who shares in the government and also submits to be governed. Their condition varies depending on the state; the best is when a person can choose and consistently pursue virtue throughout their entire life, both in their public and private lives. Such men are not the object of law, for they are themselves a law. It would be ridiculous for anyone to try to include them in the penalties of a law, as they might argue that lions and hares, who were said by Antisthenes to have demanded an equal share in government with each other, could not possibly be subject to the same laws as everyone else. For what reason do those who are highly esteemed for their power, wealth, social connections, or any other factor that gives them undue influence over government, submit to ostracism and leave the city for a specified period? This practice is said to have originated with the Argonauts, who refused to take Hercules on board their ship Argo due to his exceptional bravery.

It is fitting that we shift our discussion to explore the nature of monarchies, as we have already acknowledged their place among forms of government that are inherently sound. The one is guarded by its citizens, and the other is protected from them. In ancient times, the power of kings extended to everything, both civil, domestic, and foreign. However, in later times, they relinquished some of their privileges, and others were assumed by the people. As a result, in some states, kings were left with only the right to preside over sacrifices. Even those who were worth calling kings had only the right to command in their foreign wars.

Laws possess a quality that is inherent to the human soul. The king who possesses the power will never allow such children to inherit his throne. It is argued that taking an instance from the arts is flawed. It is said that it would be wrong for a sick person to seek relief in books, but rather that they should employ skilled physicians who earn their living by curing the sick. In contrast, those in charge of public affairs often act out of hatred or favour, rather than reason.

It is evident from what has been said that among equals, it is neither advantageous nor right for one person to be lord over all where there are no established laws, with their will as the law. Alternatively, it is not right for a good person to have authority over those who are also good, or for a bad person to have authority over those who are also bad. Nor is it right for someone superior in worth to have authority except in a particular manner that shall be described, although this point has already been mentioned. It is necessary to know what sort of government is best suited for all cities. Most writers who have addressed this subject, despite their flaws in handling other aspects, have failed to describe the practical parts. It is not enough to recognize what is best if it cannot be implemented. He, therefore, who aspires to the character of a legislator, should also be able to correct the mistakes of an existing government.

All these states have fallen into excess. He should not have said that one oligarchy was better than another, but rather that they were not as bad as they seemed. There must therefore necessarily be as many different forms of government as there are different social classes, arising from the superiority of some over others, and their varying circumstances. A government where the supreme power is held by a few should not be called an oligarchy. Let us consider a scenario where there are 1,300 people, with 1,000 of them being wealthy. Would it be fair to deny the three remaining poor individuals any say in the government, even though they were free and equal in every other aspect? It would be unreasonable to claim that such a system is democratic. The rich would not form a democracy because they are superior in number. In the past, at Colophon, this was true; however, there the majority had large possessions before the Lydian war. A democracy is a state where the freemen and the poor, being the majority, hold power.

The seventh sort are those who serve the public in expensive employments at their own charge. These are called the wealthy. The eighth are those who execute the different offices of the state. Without these, it could not possibly subsist. It is therefore necessary that there should be some persons capable of governing and filling the places in the city. This can either be for life or in rotation. The office of senator and judge, which we have already discussed, are the only ones remaining. There are many different kinds of ordinary people, as well as those considered gentlemen. Any one, therefore, may with great justice blame such a government as being a democracy, and not a free state. For where the government is not in the laws, there can be no free state. The law ought to be supreme over all things; particular incidents that arise should be determined by the magistrates or the state.

If, therefore, a democracy is to be considered a free state, it is clear that any such establishment that concentrates all power in the votes of the people cannot, by definition, be a democracy. Their decrees cannot be universal in scope. CHAPTER V
Of different species of oligarchies, one is when the right to offices is regulated by a certain census. This means that even though the poor may make up the majority, they have no share in holding power. On the other hand, all those who are included within this census take part in managing public affairs. Moreover, there are other states that share the same name, yet differ from oligarchies and free states. In these regimes, both the wealthy and virtuous have a stake in governance, earning them the title of aristocracies. It is only in governments where virtue is not a primary concern that individuals of worth and proven goodness can still be found.

CHAPTER VIII
It now remains for us to discuss the government commonly referred to as a free state and tyranny. I have chosen to address this topic first because it shares similarities with the aristocracies previously mentioned. Although these systems may not appear excessive, they have strayed from the ideal of a perfect government. They are deviations both equally from other forms, as I said at the beginning. It is fitting to acknowledge a tyranny as the most despotic form of government, for it is the only one that lacks the characteristics of true governance. However, my intention is to discuss all forms of government in general, and this concept will be addressed in its proper context.

There are three different ways in which two states may be blended and joined together. In the first place, all those rules that have been ordered by each of them can be adopted. For example, in a judicial system, in an oligarchy, the wealthy are required to attend court as jurors if they do not, but the poor are not paid for their attendance. In contrast, in democracies, both the rich and the poor are compensated for their participation. In a democracy, the right to vote in public assemblies can either be unrestricted or limited by a very small number of citizens. In contrast, an oligarchy restricts voting rights to only those with a high census. Given these two opposing practices, it is reasonable to establish a middle ground where the number of eligible voters falls between these extremes. It now remains to discuss tyranny, not because there is much to say on the topic, but as it is an integral part of our overall plan, which includes various forms of government.

A happy life must arise from an uninterrupted course of virtue. If what I have said in my treatise on Morals is true, then virtue consists in a certain medium, and the middle life must certainly be the happiest. This medium is attainable by everyone. A city composed of such men must therefore consist of slaves and masters, not freemen. For one party must hate, and the other despise each other, where there could be no possibility of friendship or political community. Community supposes affection; for we do not even associate with our enemies on the road. It is therefore the greatest happiness which citizens can enjoy in possessing a moderate and convenient fortune. For when some possess too much, and others have nothing at all, the government must either be in the hands of the meanest rabble or become a pure oligarchy. Alternatively, from the excesses of both, a tyranny arises, often resulting from a headstrong democracy or an oligarchy, but rarely when the members of the community are nearly equal to one another.

What has been said will show us why some free states have changed to democracies, while others have become oligarchies. This happens when the middle state has too few people. In such cases, those with more power - whether the wealthy or the poor - always overpower them and take control of public affairs. There are no obvious errors or inconsistencies that require correction. CHAPTER XII After what has been said, it follows that we should now show what form of government is most suitable for particular individuals. First, let us establish a general principle: that the party seeking to maintain the existing administration of the state must always be stronger than the one advocating change.

The same spirit of legislation prevailed with respect to bearing arms and attending gymnastic exercises. For the poor, no penalty was imposed if they had no arms; however, the rich were fined for not having any. Similarly, if they failed to attend their gymnastic exercises, there was no penalty for one group, but a fine for the other. As a result, the fear of this penalty led the rich to keep their arms and attend their exercises, while the poor did neither. People can meet in a collective body for the purpose of holding comitia, making laws, determining matters related to war and peace, and investigating the conduct of their magistrates. The remaining public business is handled by the magistrates, who have separate departments and are chosen from the entire community either by vote or ballot. In an oligarchy, they should either summon some of the common people to the council or establish a court, as is done in other states. In this latter case, they are known as pre-advisers or guardians of the laws. Their duty is to propose legislation before it is enacted.

People may be allowed to vote on any proposed bill, but they cannot propose anything that contradicts it. Alternatively, they can offer their advice, while the decision-making authority remains with the magistrates. There are also individuals from prominent families who, due to their status, refuse to be on equal footing with others. They consider themselves noble because they boast about their ancestors' achievements and wealth. In reality, this is where conflicts and disturbances originate. A democracy is safer and less liable to sedition than an oligarchy. In an oligarchy, two causes can lead to sedition: either the few in power may conspire against each other or against the people. In contrast, a democracy faces only one threat: the few who seek exclusive power.

Now that I have mentioned the cause which most universally inclines men to desire a change in government, it is clear. Those who seek equality will be quick to revolt if they see those they consider equals possessing more than themselves, as well as those who are not content with equality but aspire to superiority, believing they deserve more than their inferiors have, yet are only equal or less fortunate than them. Excessive honors are also a cause of sedition when one person or more surpass the state and the power of the government can permit; for then a monarchy or dynasty is usually established. This led to the introduction of ostracism in some places, such as Argos and Athens. It is better to guard against such excesses in the founding of a state than to correct them afterward. The rich also in democracies despise disorder and anarchy that may arise. They hope to improve themselves by the same means that occurred in Thebes after the Battle of Oenophyta, where bad administration led to the downfall of democracy. Similarly, at Megara, the power of the people was lost due to anarchy and disorder. In Syracuse, a similar situation existed before Gelon's tyranny took hold. At Rhodes, sedition preceded the overthrow of the popular government.

The Antisseans, along with those who had been exiled from Chios, later followed suit. The Zancleans, too, adopted a similar approach by taking in the people of Samos. The Appollonians, living in the Euxine Sea, granted freedom to their guests after admitting them to their city. However, they faced internal strife: the Syracusians, who had recently overthrown their tyrants and enrolled foreign mercenaries among their citizens, began to quarrel with each other and eventually broke into open conflict. The people of Amphipolis, having welcomed a colony of Chalcidians, were largely forced out of their city by them. Many persons occasion seditions in oligarchies because they think themselves ill-used in not sharing the honours of the state with their equals. However, in democracies, the principal people also do the same, as they believe they should have a greater share than those who are not equal to them.

Disputes between prominent individuals can have far-reaching consequences for the entire city. In Hestiaea, a case in point is the dispute between two brothers over their paternal estate. The poorer brother, who had been cheated out of part of his inheritance by his wealthier sibling, won the support of the popular crowd. On the other hand, the richer brother, who was well-connected and fashionable, managed to sway the men of high society to his side. At Delphos, a quarrel over a wedding ceremony sparked all the subsequent unrest among them. The groom, frightened by an unfavorable omen on the day of his engagement, abandoned her without marrying her. His relatives, resenting this decision, secretly conspired to insert sacred money into his pocket during the sacrifice, and then murdered him as an impious person. In general, the beginnings and causes of seditions in all states are similar to what I have described. Revolutions occur in two ways: either through violence or deception. If by violence, it happens initially by forcing them to accept the change when it is implemented.

The same thing was brought about at Heraclea shortly after the city's settlement by the same people. The citizens of note, who had been ill-treated by them, left the city, but later joined forces and returned to overthrow the popular state. Just as this occurred at Cume, during the time of democracy, when Thrasymachus was overthrown, anyone who examines events in other city-states will see that similar revolutions arise from the same underlying causes. There are two things which of all others most evidently occasion a revolution in an oligarchy: one is when the people are ill used, for then every individual is ripe for sedition. More particularly if one of the oligarchy should happen to be their leader, as was the case with Lygdamis, who was tyrant of Naxos after being leader at Naxus. At Cnidus, the oligarchy was destroyed by the nobles quarrelling with each other because the government was in the hands of so few. In fact, if a father held office, his son could not; or, if there were many brothers, only the eldest would serve. The people took advantage of their disputes and elected one of the nobles as their general, securing a victory. This is because where there are divisions among those in power, government becomes weak. During the oligarchy of the Basilides, the state of Erithria experienced great prosperity under their effective governance. However, the people grew discontent with the concentration of power among a small group, leading them to alter the form of government.

The others are those demagogues who have a share in the oligarchy. They flatter the people to maintain their power. Such was the case with the state guardians at Larissa, who flattered the people because they had been elected by them. An oligarchy is liable to revolution both in times of war and peace. In war, distrust among citizens leads the government to hire mercenary troops, and the commander of the army often assumes tyranny, as seen at Corinth with Timophanes. If multiple generals are appointed, a dynasty will likely be established. To prevent this, oligarchs may grant the people some share in governance, fearing that otherwise they would be forced to employ them. The same event occurred at Abydos, during the time of the political clubs, in which Iphiclus' club was another.

The magistrates, known as counselors, initially joined forces with an intention to oppose it but eventually agreed to it due to their belief that if the law was not repealed, they would retain control over all other public affairs. However, when they attempted to curb further changes being made, they found themselves unable to do so, as the entire form of government had been transformed into a dynasty led by those who first introduced the innovations. The first thing that the state ought to guard against is nothing.
